cards.definition: A controlled experiment comparing two versions (A and B) of a single variable, with random assignment of subjects, to determine which version produces a better outcome.
The added value that a brand name gives to a product beyond its functional benefits, derived from consumer awareness, associations, perceived quality, and loyalty.
The first level of Keller's brand resonance pyramid, focused on creating deep and broad brand awareness (salience) so that consumers know who you are.
A short three- to five-word phrase that captures the irrefutable essence or spirit of a brand's positioning, structured as emotional modifier + descriptive modifier + brand function.
The second level of Keller's brand resonance pyramid, encompassing what the brand stands for through its Performance associations (functional) and Imagery associations (abstract).

The fourth and highest level of Keller's pyramid, describing the ultimate relationship a consumer has with a brand, characterized by behavioral loyalty, attitudinal attachment, community, and engagement.
The third level of Keller's brand resonance pyramid, encompassing what consumers think (Judgments) and feel (Feelings) about the brand.

The relationship where a change in one variable directly produces a change in another, as opposed to mere correlation; established through experimental methods with random assignment and controlled manipulation.
A quantitative concept evaluation method that uses weighted criteria and numerical ratings to produce a total score for each concept, enabling finer distinctions than concept screening.
A qualitative concept evaluation method that compares each concept to a reference concept on multiple criteria using +/0/- ratings to quickly narrow down a large set of concepts.
A range of values within which the true population parameter falls with a specified probability (typically 95%); used to assess precision and significance of experimental results.
A multivariate statistical technique that determines how consumers value different product attributes by analyzing their preferences across product profiles with varying attribute combinations.
A type of new product that provides similar performance to existing products but at a lower cost, achieved through redesign, materials substitution, or manufacturing process improvements.
The longest sequence of dependent activities in a project network that determines the minimum project duration; any delay to a critical path activity delays the entire project.
A structured expression of what customers require from a product, translated from raw customer voice data into a format that can guide product specification development.
An experimental design that tests two or more factors simultaneously at multiple levels, allowing assessment of both main effects and interaction effects.
The point in the product development process at which product specifications are locked and no further changes are accepted; requires balancing information quality against time-to-market pressure.
A framework that defines market opportunities based on the underlying task or job customers are trying to accomplish, rather than focusing on product features or customer demographics.

A four-level brand-building framework progressing from Identity (salience) through Meaning (performance and imagery) and Response (judgments and feelings) to Resonance (loyalty, attachment, community, engagement).
An addition to an existing product line offered to the firm's current markets, designed to provide additional variety, fill gaps, or preempt competitors within an established category.
The process of estimating the total potential demand (in revenue or units) for a product within a defined market, using top-down, bottom-up, or analogous estimation approaches.
A product architecture approach where a product is composed of independent, interchangeable modules that can be mixed and matched to create different product variants, enabling mass customization.
A graphical representation of project activities and their dependencies, used to identify the critical path, calculate slack times, and determine minimum project duration.
A product that creates an entirely new category or market; the highest-risk, highest-potential type of new product, representing true innovation.

The probability of observing results as extreme as the observed data assuming no true effect exists; values below 0.05 typically indicate statistical significance and rejection of the null hypothesis.
The numerical value assigned to a specific level of a product attribute in conjoint analysis, representing the contribution of that attribute level to overall consumer preference.
A macro-environmental analysis framework examining Political, Economic, Social, and Technological factors that affect all industries and may create or eliminate product opportunities.

An industry analysis framework assessing competitive intensity through five forces: threat of new entrants, bargaining power of suppliers, bargaining power of buyers, threat of substitutes, and rivalry.
A written description of a proposed new product specifying its form, the technology it employs, and the benefits it delivers to a target customer.
The detailed technical and functional requirements that define what a product must do and how it must perform, translated from customer needs statements.
The practice of reducing a critical path activity's duration by adding resources at additional cost, applied to the activity with the lowest crash cost per unit of time saved.
A statistical method that models the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables; used for conjoint analysis estimation and interpretation of experimental results.
The percentage measure of how much influence each product attribute has on consumer preference, calculated as the range of part-worth utilities divided by the sum of all attribute ranges.
An existing product that is targeted at a new market segment or positioned differently in consumers' minds, without fundamental changes to the product itself.
A three-question screening tool for evaluating new product opportunities: Is it Real? Can we Win? Is it Worth it? Each question must be affirmatively answered to proceed with development.
A comprehensive assessment of the external environment (general and industry-specific) to identify opportunities and threats for new product development.
The amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying overall project completion, calculated as Latest Start minus Earliest Start; critical path activities have zero slack.
A result that is unlikely to have occurred by chance alone, typically indicated by a p-value below 0.05 or a confidence interval that does not include zero.
A hierarchical decomposition of total project scope into smaller, manageable activities or work packages, each with estimated durations and defined dependencies.
Line extensions that flesh out the product line in current markets, such as new flavors or variants of an existing product (e.g., Kellogg's Special K Red Berries).

More than a product; a brand has dimensions that differentiate it from other products, built through consumer perceptions, expectations, and experiences. Branding is the process of de-commoditizing a product.
The intrinsic value a brand holds over its competitors, cultivated by brand managers through image building and consumer relationship management.
An organizational role responsible for building and sustaining brand equity by cultivating brand image and worth; serves as an advocate of the organization.

A framework (Crawford Fig. 2.3) showing how product concepts progress from initial "opportunity concept" through increasing stages of clarity and market value until reaching "successful concept" status.
New products that provide the customer similar performance but at a lower cost, which may be "new" primarily in terms of design or production process.
The process by which branding transforms a generic product category into differentiated offerings, allowing firms to compete on factors beyond price.
The plan or specification for the implementation of a process; the third step in the Vision-Strategy-Design-Execution chain.
The carrying out of a plan; the final step in the Vision-Strategy-Design-Execution chain where products are actually built and brought to market.
A six-phase product development framework (Ulrich et al.): Planning, Concept Development, System-Level Design, Detail Design, Testing and Refinement, and Production Ramp-Up, with gate reviews.
Products that take a firm into a category new to it, though the category itself may already exist (e.g., Grab entering financial services with GrabFinance).
Also called "really-new" products; inventions that create a whole new market (e.g., the original iPhone, Netflix streaming). These represent the highest degree of product newness.
A sequence of activities that transforms a set of inputs to outputs.
Anything that can be offered to a market for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption that might satisfy a need or want; can be a physical good, service, or other offering.

The sequence of activities an organization takes to conceive, design, and commercialize a product; it is intellectual and organizational. A well-defined PD process assures quality and promotes coordination.
A cross-functional role responsible for the life cycle of a product or product line, driving vision, strategy, design, and execution. Responsible for both success and failure of their products.
A variant of the generic PD process that includes many iteration cycles of Design-Build-Test between System-Level Design and Production Ramp-Up, suitable for software and digital products.
A type of new product strategy where existing products are retargeted for a new use, application, or target market (e.g., Arm & Hammer baking soda repositioned as a refrigerator deodorizer).
A variant of the generic PD process that cycles through Planning, Concept Design, System-Level Design, Detailed Design, and Integration & Test in expanding spirals with increasing cost.
The plan of action to achieve the long-term goal (vision); the second step in the Vision-Strategy-Design-Execution chain.

A framework stating that successful innovation must simultaneously satisfy three criteria: people desirability (customers want it), business viability (the business model works), and technical feasibility.
What an organization desires to achieve in the long run; the aspirational end-state that guides all product decisions and the first step in the Vision-Strategy-Design-Execution chain.

decks.title: Lecture 2: New Product Development Part 1 - Opportunity Identification and Concept Generation

cards.definition: A five-step process for identifying and interpreting customer needs: Define scope, Gather information, Interpret customer data, Quantify relative importance, Reflect on the process.
Customer needs that are easy to express, widely known and understood, and likely already fulfilled by existing products.
The standard product development process model (Ulrich et al.): Planning, Concept Development, System-Level Design, Detail Design, Testing and Refinement, Production Ramp-Up.
The three simultaneous requirements for successful product innovation: People (desirable), Business (viable), and Technical (feasible).
Customer needs that customers may not be aware of and are unable to express. When addressed, products may delight and exceed expectations.
The process of estimating total sales of a product or service, typically over one year. Uses the filter formula: Market Size = # units x Average Price/unit.
A formalized expression of a customer need, written following specific guidelines (what not how, specific, positive, product attribute, avoid must/should) to enable translation into specifications.
The sequence of activities to conceive, design, and commercialize a product. Five phases: Opportunity Identification, Concept Generation, Concept/Project Evaluation, Development, and Launch.
An idea for a new product; a product description in embryonic form, a newly sensed need, a newly discovered technology, or a rough match between a need and a possible solution.
A risk mitigation strategy that passes a large pool of raw opportunities through multiple filters until only a limited number of exceptional opportunities remain.
A framework for analyzing the general environment using six factors: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, and Legal.
A framework for evaluating industry attractiveness through five competitive forces: threat of new entrants, bargaining power of buyers/suppliers, threat of substitutes, and intensity of rivalry.
A measurable description of a product requirement, composed of a Metric + Value(s) + Units (e.g., Total Mass: 8.5-10 kg).
A PD process variant that incorporates many iteration cycles of Design-Build-Test between system-level design and production ramp-up.
A framework by Day (2007) for evaluating innovation opportunities by asking three questions: Is it real? Can we win? Is it worth it? Maps to the three innovation challenges.
The portion of TAM that a business can realistically target with its current offerings, considering geography, product scope, and price tier.
The share of SAM that a business can capture considering competition and execution constraints. Shows "what you can credibly win."
A PD process variant where development spirals outward through planning, concept design, system-level design, detailed design, and integration & test, with cumulative effort increasing each pass.
The entire potential revenue opportunity for a product or service in a specific market, assuming 100% market share. Shows "how big the dream is."
Customer needs that are easy for customers to express but are known to be difficult to address and are generally not yet fulfilled.

decks.title: Lecture 3: New Product Development Part 2 - Concept Screening and Testing

cards.definition: The practice of evaluating competitive products on both perceived satisfaction of customer needs and established metrics to set informed target specifications.
The advantage to the customer for which they perceive a need or desire; one of the three inputs for defining a product concept and typically the least risky starting point.
A rigorous quantitative evaluation tool that scores concepts on weighted criteria (typically 1-5 scale) to produce ranked total scores for final concept selection.
A rapid first-pass evaluation tool that compares multiple concepts against a reference concept using simple +/0/- ratings on unweighted criteria to narrow a large set of alternatives.
A stage of elimination in the NPD process where concepts are evaluated against the PIC criteria and excluded if they require unavailable technologies, target unfamiliar customers, or fail on cost.
A structured expression of a customer need written following specific guidelines (what not how, specific, positive, product attribute, avoiding must/should) to enable translation into specifications.
A deep qualitative research method involving immersion into the customer's environment and culture to discover latent needs that cannot be surfaced through interviews or surveys alone.
The physical thing created or the set of processes by which a service is delivered; one of the three inputs required to define a product concept.
The aspirational or best-in-class target for a product specification, representing breakthrough performance on a given metric.
A framework asserting that customers "hire" products to accomplish a specific job, expressed as: "When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [desired outcome]."
The minimum acceptable performance level for a product specification; falling below this threshold means the product fails to meet customer expectations.
A concept selection method where concepts are tested with real customers using experiments or pilots; offers high validity but is costly and time-consuming.
A quantitative or qualitative measure used to assess how well a product meets a specific customer need (e.g., mass in kg, time to lock in seconds, comfort as a subjective rating).
A concept selection method where team members vote openly on preferred concepts; fast and energizing but prone to groupthink and dominance effects.
A qualitative concept selection method that compares concepts by listing advantages versus drawbacks or benefits versus required effort.
A verbal or prototype statement describing what will be changed in a product and how the customer stands to gain or lose. Requires at least two of three inputs: form, technology, and benefit/need.
A measurable description of a product requirement consisting of three components: a metric, a value (or range), and units (e.g., Total Mass = 8.5-10 kg).
A concept selection method using anonymous voting to reduce social pressure and hierarchy bias; useful when opinions are sensitive or conflict is likely.
The point at which product specifications are locked for downstream development. Early freeze enables optimization; late freeze enables better market matching.
The source or means by which the product's form is attained; one of the three inputs for defining a product concept.

decks.title: Lecture 4: Concept Testing Review and Guest Lecture

cards.definition: A method of comparing two versions of a product or feature by exposing different user groups to each version and measuring which performs better. Used during production ramp-up.
Internal performance and user interface testing conducted during the detail design phase, typically by the development team or internal users.
Validation and acceptance testing conducted with real users in real-world conditions, typically during the testing and refinement phase before full launch.
A structured group ideation technique designed to generate a large quantity of creative ideas by following rules such as deferring judgment, building on others' ideas, and encouraging wild thinking.
A quantitative evaluation method that rates product concepts against weighted criteria to produce total weighted scores and rankings, enabling systematic comparison and selection.
A structured table used to evaluate multiple product concepts against weighted selection criteria, producing ranked scores that guide concept selection and refinement.
The initial stage of concept selection that uses simple scales (+, 0, -) to compare concepts against a reference concept and quickly eliminate weak candidates.
Phase 3 of the product development process in which selected concepts are evaluated for feasibility, desirability, and strategic fit before advancing to full development. A stage of elimination.
A formal checkpoint in the product development process where stakeholders decide whether a project should proceed to the next stage, be revised, or be terminated.
A strategic document that defines the boundaries of a product development effort, including target markets, technology requirements, and innovation goals. Used as a filter during concept testing.
Design iteration testing conducted during concept development and system-level design phases, using physical or digital prototypes to validate design decisions.
A benchmark product concept used as the standard against which all other concepts are compared during screening and scoring evaluations.
The practice of testing how changes in importance weightings or uncertain ratings affect the concept ranking, ensuring the selected concept is robust to assumption changes.
Benchmarking tests conducted early in the product development process to verify that product specifications meet defined standards.
The product of a criterion's importance weight and a concept's rating on that criterion, used to compute total concept scores in a scoring matrix.

decks.title: Lecture 5: Conjoint Analysis for Product Design

cards.definition: An important product characteristic that is varied in a conjoint analysis study (e.g., thickness, spiciness, price). Each attribute has multiple levels.
The reference level for each attribute that is omitted from the regression equation; all other levels are interpreted relative to it (e.g., Regular thickness, Mild spiciness).
A variant of conjoint analysis where respondents choose their preferred option from sets of product profiles rather than ranking or rating all profiles.
The estimated numerical value in a regression equation that quantifies the effect of an attribute level on the dependent variable, holding all other attributes constant.
A survey-based statistical technique to determine how people value different attributes of a product or service, based on the assumption that product value equals the sum of attribute values.
A production strategy that postpones the differentiating steps of manufacturing until customer preferences or demand signals are known, reducing inventory risk and waste.
The practice of converting categorical attribute levels into binary (0/1) variables for regression analysis, with one level per attribute designated as the baseline and omitted.
An experimental design approach that tests only a strategically chosen subset of all possible attribute combinations, used when the full factorial would require too many profiles.
A conjoint analysis design where respondents evaluate every possible combination of attribute levels (e.g., 3x3x2 = 18 salsa profiles).
The specific quantities or qualities that an attribute can take in a conjoint analysis. Each attribute has two or more levels representing the options being tested.
A production strategy combining the efficiency of mass production with the flexibility of individual customization, typically enabled by modular design and postponement.
A design approach where a product is composed of discrete, interchangeable modules with standardized interfaces, allowing independent upgrade, replacement, or customization of individual modules.
The probability that the observed regression coefficient could have arisen by chance if the true effect were zero. A p-value below 0.05 is conventionally considered statistically significant.

A Japanese term meaning "mistake-proofing" -- a fail-safe design method that builds safeguards into products or processes to prevent user errors (e.g., battery compartments shaped for correct orientation).
The arrangement of functional elements into physical chunks (modules) which become the building blocks for the product or product family.
The proportion of variance in the dependent variable explained by the regression model, ranging from 0 (no explanatory power) to 1 (perfect fit).

A statistical method to estimate the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables. In conjoint analysis, it decomposes preference into attribute-level contributions.
The percentage contribution of each attribute to overall preference, calculated as the range of an attribute's coefficients divided by the sum of ranges across all attributes.
An alternative name for conjoint analysis, emphasizing that respondents trade off less desirable attribute levels for more desirable ones when evaluating product profiles.
The value that customers place on each level of an attribute, estimated from regression coefficients in conjoint analysis. Higher utility indicates greater preference contribution.
The maximum additional amount a consumer would pay for a specific attribute level relative to the baseline while remaining equally satisfied, calculated from attribute and price coefficient ratios.
A controlled experiment comparing two versions of a single variable (e.g., webpage design, email subject line) to determine which performs better on a defined metric.
The smallest unit of work effort in a project that consumes both time and resources, and can be scheduled and controlled by the project manager.
The maximum length of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the entire project's completion. Calculated as LS - ES or LF - EF. Critical activities have zero slack.
An extraneous variable that correlates with both the independent and dependent variables, potentially creating a spurious apparent relationship between them.
The hypothetical outcome that would have occurred in the absence of a treatment or intervention. Since counterfactuals cannot be directly observed, control groups serve as proxies.
An activity on the critical path with zero slack. Any delay to a critical activity directly delays the entire project.
The longest path through a project network diagram, determining the minimum project duration. All activities on this path have zero slack.
The earliest time at which an activity can be completed, calculated as ES + Activity Duration during the forward pass.
The earliest time at which an activity can begin, determined by the maximum EF of all its immediate predecessors.
A cognitive bias where people ascribe higher value to objects they own compared to equivalent objects they do not own, leading to a gap between willingness to accept and willingness to pay.
An experimental design in which all possible combinations of two or more independent variables are tested, allowing researchers to assess both main effects and interaction effects.
The latest time at which an activity can finish without delaying the project, calculated during the backward pass as the minimum LS of all immediate successors.
The latest time at which an activity can start without delaying the project, calculated as LF - Activity Duration.
A visual representation of project activities and their precedence relationships, typically using Activity-on-Node (AON) notation, where nodes represent activities and arrows show dependencies.
A sequence of connected activities that leads from the starting node to the finishing node in a network diagram.
Program Evaluation and Review Technique / Critical Path Method -- scheduling techniques used to plan and control project activities by identifying the critical path and calculating activity timing.
The process of reducing the duration of critical path activities by allocating additional resources, typically at increased cost, to shorten the overall project timeline.
An experimental design in which participants are randomly assigned to treatment and control groups, considered the gold standard for establishing causal effects.
An indication that an observed result is unlikely to have occurred by chance alone (conventionally, p < 0.05), providing confidence that the effect is real rather than random variation.
A hierarchical decomposition of a project's total scope into progressively smaller, manageable work packages, organized by levels from the overall project down to individual tasks.
A theoretical model of memory where knowledge is stored as interconnected nodes (concepts) and links (associations) of varying strength. Applied to branding, it describes consumer "mental maps."
The extent to which consumers can identify or recognize a brand under different conditions. Comprises brand recognition (aided) and brand recall (unaided retrieval from memory).
A trademarked device that identifies and differentiates a brand, including names, logos, symbols, characters, slogans, jingles, and packaging. Must be memorable, meaningful, and protectable.
The differential effect that brand knowledge has on consumer response to the marketing of a brand. Positive equity exists when consumers react more favorably when they can identify the brand.
The set of perceptions and associations about a brand held in consumer memory. Characterized by the strength, favorability, and uniqueness of brand associations.
A three- to five-word articulation of the brand's essence, consisting of an emotional modifier, a descriptive modifier, and a brand function. Used internally to guide marketing decisions.

A building block of the Brand Resonance Pyramid (Meaning level, rational side) describing how well the product meets customers' functional needs, including reliability, serviceability, style, and price.

The act of designing the company's offering and image to occupy a distinctive place in the target consumer's mind, defined by target market, competitive frame, points of parity, and points of difference.
The consumer's ability to retrieve a brand from memory when given a product category, usage situation, or need as a cue, without the brand being physically present. Higher bar than recognition.
The consumer's ability to confirm prior exposure to a brand when given the brand as a cue (e.g., seeing a logo and confirming familiarity). A lower bar than brand recall.
The highest level of the Brand Resonance Pyramid, representing the ultimate relationship between consumer and brand. Characterized by loyalty, attachment, community, and active engagement.
A four-level, six-block framework for building strong brands. From bottom to top: Identity (Salience), Meaning (Performance, Imagery), Response (Judgments, Feelings), Relationships (Resonance).
The depth and breadth of brand awareness -- how easily, frequently, and in how many situations the brand comes to mind. The foundation of the Brand Resonance Pyramid.
Strong, favorable, and unique brand associations that consumers believe they cannot find to the same extent with a competitive brand. They form the basis of competitive advantage.
Brand associations shared with other brands that are not necessarily unique, but are necessary for the brand to be considered a legitimate and credible competitor in the category.
The four-step process of (1) establishing brand positioning/values, (2) planning/implementing brand marketing programs, (3) measuring brand performance, and (4) growing/sustaining brand equity.

decks.title: Lecture 8: Brand Sustainability and Personal Branding

cards.definition: The weakening of a brand's core identity and perceived value that can occur when a brand extends into too many categories or becomes too widely accessible.
A short phrase capturing the essence of an individual's professional identity, structured as emotional modifier + descriptive modifier + brand function.
The strategic process of changing a brand's position in consumers' minds relative to competitors, which can range from incremental adjustments to disruptive overhauls.
The use of product purchases and brand choices to communicate social status, identity, values, or group membership to others.
In the context of points-of-difference for personal branding, the extent to which a person can actually and consistently demonstrate or perform the skills and abilities they claim.
In personal branding, the degree to which a person's passion, values, and brand promise resonate with and are wanted by their target audience.
In personal branding, the long-term viability and uniqueness of a person's positioning relative to competitors in their professional space.
A bold, fundamental shift in a brand's market positioning that breaks significantly from its historical identity, as opposed to incremental or evolutionary changes.
The perception that a brand or product is rare, limited, or accessible only to a select group, which is a core value driver in the luxury industry.
The strategic risk created when a disproportionate share of a brand's revenue comes from consumers of a single country or region.
The underlying motivations for consumers to pay premium prices for luxury goods, including status signaling, quality perception, self-reward, craftsmanship appreciation, and social belonging.
A visual diagram showing all the associations and perceptions that people hold about a brand (or person), displayed as attributes radiating outward from a central brand node.
The assortment of product categories and styles that a brand offers, which in luxury must be carefully managed to balance breadth of appeal with brand coherence.
The evolved model of luxury consumption characterized by experience over ownership, digital engagement, younger consumers, and values-driven purchasing.
The traditional model of luxury consumption characterized by status symbolism, exclusive in-store experiences, ownership-focused consumption, and resistance to digital channels.
A visual tool that plots brands or products on two or more dimensions (e.g., classic vs. contemporary, demographic appeal) to illustrate competitive positioning.
The practice of applying brand management principles -- including positioning, differentiation, brand mantra, and mental maps -- to build and manage an individual's professional identity.
A self-assessment tool that evaluates an individual's Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats as part of the personal branding process.
The unique attributes or benefits that distinguish an individual from competitors in the job market or professional space, evaluated on desirability, deliverability, and differentiation.
The deliberate limitation of product supply to maintain perceptions of exclusivity and drive premium pricing, commonly used in the luxury industry.
In personal branding, the extent to which the market (employers, clients, partners) will actually reward and value a person's chosen brand positioning.
